MDX and React

MDX and React

Section titled “MDX and React”

Docusaurus has built-in support for MDX , which allows you to write JSX within your Markdown files and render them as React components .

Docusaurus v3 uses MDX v3 , so the parser is stricter than the old MDX v1 pipeline.

Debugging MDX

Check out the MDX docs to see what other fancy stuff you can do with MDX.

Use the MDX playground to validate strict MDX v3 syntax when something doesn’t compile.

Exporting components

Section titled “Exporting components”

To define any custom component within an MDX file, you have to export it

Only paragraphs that start with export will be parsed as components instead of prose.

Note

prose : written or spoken language in its ordinary form

Example

MDX is JSX

Since all doc files are parsed using MDX, anything that looks like HTML is actually JSX.

Therefore, if you need to inline-style a component, follow JSX flavor and provide style objects.

Importing components

Section titled “Importing components”

You can also import your own components defined in other files or third-party components installed via npm.

Tip

The @site alias points to your website’s directory , usually where the docusaurus.config.js file is .

While declaring components within Markdown is very convenient for simple cases, it becomes hard to maintain because of limited editor support, risks of parsing errors, and low reusability.

Use a separate .js file when your component involves complex JS logic:

Importing Markdown

Section titled “Importing Markdown”

You can use Markdown files as components and import them elsewhere, either in Markdown files or in React pages .

By convention, using the _ filename prefix will not create any doc page and means the Markdown file is a “partial” , to be imported by other files.

Importing in other Markdown files (.mdx)

Section titled “Importing in other Markdown files (.mdx)”

Importing in other JSX files

Section titled “Importing in other JSX files”

If you are importing Markdown in a React page , you have to supply MDXContent provider yourself.

Note

Wrap your imported Markdown component with MDXContent provider.

Caution

Currently, the table of contents does not contain the imported Markdown headings.

Importing code snippets

Section titled “Importing code snippets”

You can also import any code file as raw text , and then insert it in a code block .

You need to install Webpack raw-loader in your project:

  • npm
  • yarn

Now you can import code snippets from another file as it is:

See also

See using code blocks in JSX for more details of the <CodeBlock> component.

MDX component scope

Section titled “MDX component scope”

Apart from importing a component and exporting a component, a third way to use a component in MDX is to register it to the global scope , which will make it automatically available in every MDX file , without any import statements.

For example, given this MDX file:

It will be compiled to a React component containing ul, li, p, and YourCustomComponent tags.

In Docusaurus, this MDX component scope is provided by the @theme/MDXComponents component.

It’s not a React component, per se, unlike most other exports under the @theme/ alias: it is a record from tag names like ul and img to their custom implementations.

If you want to register extra tag names (like the <YourCustomComponent> tag above), you should consider wrapping @theme/MDXComponents, so you don’t have to maintain all the existing mappings.

Since the swizzle CLI doesn’t allow wrapping non-component files yet, you should manually create the wrapper:

And now, you can freely use <highlight> in every page, without writing the import statement:

Available exports

Section titled “Available exports”

​ Within the MDX page, the following variables are available as globals:

  • frontMatter: the front matter as a record of string keys and values;
  • toc: the table of contents, as a tree of headings.
  • contentTitle: the Markdown title, which is the first h1 heading in the Markdown text. It’s undefined if there isn’t one (e.g. title specified in the front matter).

Sources

Section titled “Sources”
  • https://docusaurus.io/docs/markdown-features/react

Last updated: Sep 28, 2025
